lesson: A level that can be completed by holding one direction with zero enemies, hazards, or elevation changes fails to engage players — traversal alone without meaningful interaction (platforming, combat, or verticality) is insufficient for fun, regardless of polish.
conditions: level can be beaten by holding one direction
zero enemies or hazards present
exit at same elevation as spawn with no intervening obstacles
fast completion time with zero deaths/falls
pit depth exceeds max jump height
no wall-kick or climb surfaces
exit located above pit
outcome: players rate level poorly (1-3/5) despite functional mechanics; perceived as 'walk simulator' or 'flat' rather than a platformer

merged_from.lesson: Fast completion time with zero deaths/falls signals the level was too easy/under-stimulating rather than well-tuned.
A level that can be beaten by holding one direction with zero player inputs beyond movement provides no engagement and will always rate poorly regardless of polish.
Exit placement at the same elevation as spawn with no intervening obstacles turns the level into a walk simulator rather than a platformer.
Deep, unclimbable pits at the bottom of a level act as soft locks — players perceive them as unfair traps rather than skill challenges when no recovery path exists.

lesson: Enemy variety without terrain variety still yields low engagement — enemies need diverse spatial contexts (verticality, cover, chokepoints, elevation changes) to create distinct encounters; flat levels cannot provide engaging combat or platforming.
conditions: level has no vertical traversal (climbing/descending)
enemies present but placed on flat horizontal plane
aerial enemies positioned above player's projectile arc
ladder height exceeds ~8-10 tiles
ladders are the sole traversal method
no horizontal ledges, rest platforms, or branching paths along the climb
no alternative traversal paths (wall-kick, moving platforms, etc.)
vertical shaft exceeds ~10 tiles height
no horizontal ledges, platforms, or branching paths along the climb
ladder height > 8 tiles
no horizontal ledges or rest platforms on climb
fall locations cluster at same ladder x-position
outcome: players rate level poorly (1-3/5); combat feels unfair or trivial, platforming feels absent; fun and verticality judged as separate axes

merged_from.lesson: Flat levels with no vertical traversal (climbing/descending) and poorly positioned aerial enemies fail to deliver engaging platforming or combat.
Enemy variety without terrain variety still yields low engagement — enemies need diverse spatial contexts (verticality, cover, chokepoints) to create distinct encounters.

Long uninterrupted ladder climbs exceeding ~8-10 tiles without rest platforms, horizontal ledges, or alternative traversal paths cause player fatigue and repeated falls at the same height, turning vertical traversal into a punishment loop rather than a skill challenge.

Ladder climbs that exceed 8 tiles in height and lack horizontal ledges, rest platforms, or alternative paths become fatigue traps — players fall repeatedly at the same height, lose all progress, and perceive the climb as unfair rather than skillful.

lesson: Horizontal floor gaps/pits are not perceived as verticality by players even if platforms sit at different heights — true verticality needs traversal that changes the player's elevation (climbing/descending), not just gaps at one baseline.
Flying enemies placed above the player's maximum projectile arc create unfair, uncounterable threats that frustrate rather than challenge.
An exit placed on a high platform without climbable paths or vertical traversal options renders the goal unreachable, turning exploration into guesswork.

Stationary shooters placed on the same horizontal plane as the player's movement path become harmless if the player can pass beneath their firing arc — vertical offset or elevation change is required to make them threatening.

merged_from.lesson: Turrets or stationary shooters placed on wide horizontal platforms become harmless if the player can pass beneath their firing arc — elevation or vertical offset is required for threat relevance.

Stationary shooters placed on the same elevation as the player's movement path become irrelevant if the player can pass beneath their firing line — vertical offset or elevation change is required to make them threatening.

lesson: Repeating identical enemy-and-platform patterns without variation in spacing, height, or timing creates predictable, low-engagement encounters that players optimize away.
conditions: identical platform-enemy clusters repeat at fixed spacing
no variation in spacing, height, or timing
no elevation change or structural breaks between clusters
long horizontal traversal precedes final vertical section
no mid-level checkpoints or safe zones before finale
final section requires repeated precise climbs
outcome: player traverses quickly with minimal input variation, backtracks on flat sections, rates level poorly for repetition

merged_from.lesson: Repeating identical platform-and-turret patterns without variation in spacing, height, or timing create predictable, low-engagement encounters that players optimize away.

Vertical challenge clusters near the level end amplify perceived difficulty spikes — players who traverse long horizontal stretches expect the finale to test mastery, not introduce new traversal demands without checkpoints.

lesson: Terrain variance requires distinct spatial contexts — gaps, platforms at multiple elevations, ceilings, and chokepoints — not just a continuous height gradient or single repeated macro-structure (e.g., stair-step hills, continuous slope), which creates mechanical fatigue even with local geometry variation.
conditions: level composed of single repeated macro-structure
no distinct spatial contexts (gaps, multi-elevation platforms, ceilings, chokepoints)
continuous height gradient without traversal variety
level width > 200 tiles
no mid-level checkpoints or safe zones
falls occur in second half of level
respawn returns player to start
enemy dies but its projectile/hazard remains active
player can be damaged by remnant hazard after kill
no visual cue that hazard persists
outcome: mechanical fatigue, disengagement, poor ratings despite functional local geometry

merged_from.lesson: A level composed of a single repeated macro-structure (e.g., one continuous slope) creates mechanical fatigue even if local geometry varies slightly.
Terrain variance requires distinct spatial contexts — gaps, platforms at multiple elevations, ceilings, and chokepoints — not just a continuous height gradient.

When a level's horizontal span exceeds 200 tiles without mid-level checkpoints or safe zones, any fall in the latter half forces players to re-traverse 5+ minutes of content, converting challenge into tedium and causing disengagement.
Persistent projectiles or hazards from dead enemies (e.g., stingers, bullets) that remain active after the enemy is killed create perceived unfairness, as players expect threat removal on kill.

When a level's horizontal span exceeds 200 tiles without mid-level checkpoints, any fall in the latter half forces players to re-traverse 5+ minutes of content, converting challenge into tedium and causing disengagement.

lesson: Collision transitions between sloped ramps and flat ceilings produce unexpected falls that feel unfair rather than challenging.
Spawn points must be validated for clear vertical and horizontal clearance — at minimum one empty tile above and to each side — before a level is considered playable.
A level that prevents the first player input (movement/jump) fails before any design intent can be evaluated; structural validity is a prerequisite for fun, not a result of it.

Horizontal length without structural variety (elevation changes, vertical traversal mechanics, varied encounter types) creates a one-dimensional grind that causes mechanical fatigue, late-level falls, and disengagement — even fair individual jumps/enemies cannot prevent this when repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles.
conditions: level width greatly exceeds height (hundreds of tiles)
no significant elevation changes over long stretches
no ladders, climbable walls, or vertical traversal mechanics
single jump archetype or encounter pattern repeats at high density
player spawns at bottom with exit at similar elevation
level width greatly exceeds height
repeating enemy/platform patterns
one jump type dominates traversal
repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles
outcome: player falls from fatigue deep in level, spends 11+ minutes without completing, rates experience 1-2/5 despite functional mechanics in isolation

merged_from.lesson: When a single jump archetype (e.g., a fixed-height gap or climb) repeats at high density across a long horizontal stretch, it creates mechanical fatigue that manifests as late-level falls and disengagement, even if the jump is fair in isolation.

A level that spans hundreds of tiles horizontally but offers no way to climb or descend forces the player into a one-dimensional grind; even fair jumps and enemies cannot prevent disengagement when elevation never changes.

lesson: Horizontal length without structural variety (elevation changes, vertical traversal mechanics, varied encounter types) creates a one-dimensional grind that causes mechanical fatigue, late-level falls, and disengagement — even fair individual jumps/enemies cannot prevent this when repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles.
conditions: level width greatly exceeds height (hundreds of tiles)
no significant elevation changes over long stretches
no ladders, climbable walls, or vertical traversal mechanics
single jump archetype or encounter pattern repeats at high density
player spawns at bottom with exit at similar elevation
repetition density is high over hundreds of tiles
vertical shaft exceeds 10 tiles height
platform clusters repeat at uniform spacing
no landmarks, color cues, or structural breaks to mark the critical path
multiple enemy types placed without spatial hierarchy
level width exceeds 100 tiles
platform pattern repeats at fixed spacing
enemy types and positions repeat per cluster
no elevation change or structural breaks between clusters
outcome: player falls from fatigue deep in level, spends 11+ minutes without completing, rates experience 1-2/5 despite functional mechanics in isolation

merged_from.lesson: Repeating identical platform-enemy clusters at uniform horizontal intervals creates a predictable rhythm that players optimize away, eliminating engagement even when individual jumps or enemies are functional in isolation.
